title: "Freedomland U.S.A." layout: page.njk permalink: /stories/freedomland/

One of the most magical places I ever visited as a child was Freedomland U.S.A., a sprawling theme park in the Bronx that opened in 1960 and was unlike anything I had ever seen. When you arrived and parked, you didn’t just walk into an amusement park — you stepped back in time, entering through Little Old New York, styled after the 1890s. The streets were alive with Model T cars, horse-drawn trolleys, and actors in period clothing. I remember shopfronts selling old-time goods and costumed performers shouting political speeches and staging fake bank robberies. It was immersive before anyone used that word. I was a kid, but I felt like I had walked into a living movie.

From the Old New York train station, you could board an authentic-looking train that looped through the park, letting you hop off at any of the seven themed regions — each one crafted with astonishing detail. I still remember how each section had its own feeling, its own climate of imagination.

In Old Chicago, you witnessed the dramatic recreation of the Great Chicago Fire. Every 20 minutes, the fire would “break out,” and visitors — kids like me — could help put it out using hand pumps. We even got firefighter certificates for our heroism. There was also a huge lake that mimicked the Great Lakes, and the Brass Rail Stockyards Restaurant, which I remember mainly for how enormous it felt.

Then there was the Old Southwest, my favorite section. It had cowboy stunt shows, where outlaws and sheriffs shot it out in the streets and fell off rooftops in dramatic slow motion. I was mesmerized. These weren’t just actors — to me, they were real cowboys. The saloon shows, the Pony Express station, the mining rides — everything fed my childhood sense of adventure.

One of the most unforgettable attractions was Casa Loco, a tilted house that threw off your sense of balance. You couldn’t walk straight — the walls and floors leaned at odd angles, making even standing still a hilarious challenge. It wasn’t a thrill ride, but it was completely disorienting in the best way.

In San Francisco, they recreated the 1906 earthquake with a dark ride that shook, creaked, and rattled as if the whole building were coming down. The Chinatown section was full of mysterious lanterns and narrow alleyways. The realism of it all left a lasting mark.

New Orleans brought a slice of Mardi Gras to the Bronx, complete with jazz music, pirates, and a Crystal Maze mirror house that felt endless when you were five. There was even a simulated tornado ride that felt genuinely scary at the time.

And somewhere in all of that — in the trains, the staged disasters, the tilted rooms and cowboy brawls — was a pure kind of American storytelling. Freedomland was ambitious. It wanted to be Disneyland, but it was grittier, rawer, and — for a young boy from New York — absolutely unforgettable.

Freedomland U.S.A. — A Lost World of Wonders

One of the most magical places I ever visited as a child was Freedomland U.S.A., a massive theme park in the Bronx shaped like a map of the United States. It was immersive, gritty, theatrical — and unforgettable.

The Seven Themed Areas of Freedomland

  • Little Old New York (1890s) – Street theater, trolley rides, Macy’s replica
  • Old Chicago (1871) – Great Fire simulation, fire pump activity for kids
  • The Great Plains (1803–1900) – Pony Express, Borden's farm with Elsie
  • San Francisco (1906) – Earthquake ride, Chinatown, railroad stop
  • The Old Southwest (1890) – Casa Loco, gunfights, mining sky-ride
  • New Orleans – Mardi Gras, Tornado ride, Crystal Maze
  • Satellite City (the future) – Rocket ship displays and space exhibits

Though it only lasted from 1960 to 1964, Freedomland’s ambition and immersive storytelling left a permanent mark on me. It was more than entertainment — it was a place where history came alive, chaos was choreographed, and wonder was engineered.
